entry.title: Re: Claude Code via MCP: Poor man's Joule, or a practical tool?
entry.content:
I won't be writing any more Blog posts, because I have already written three. Too many. I'll just comment some of my experiences here from time to time.

A short session after work today. I installed the Claude Skills published by Secondsky. 

It was interesting even just to read through them, I have learned bunch of stuff. Haven't noticed much difference before / after. Must be due to my prompting.

I would still like to be able to add my own Skills, just don't know where to put them. Simply into the Directory of the Project I am currently in?

Might work...

I'll try that.

entry.title: Re: Claude Code via MCP: Poor man's Joule, or a practical tool?
entry.content:
Tried asking an Antigravity Agent (Gemini 3.1) to explain some code from a file in the ABAP Filesystem by Marcello Urbani, that I had installed as an Extension in Antigravity. The Agent had an idea to use the vibing-steampunk binary. He queried it for options and then ran it to get the source code of the file I was pointing at:

Surely this is an overkill, I remember having achieved a similar outcome in Cursor once, before Antigravity existed and before I had any MCP Servers installed. I just don't remember how.

I'll be damned. There are SO MANY different ways of doing things here that my head is spinning.

entry.title: Released CDS View for Sales Price Condition Records with Material ID, in S/4HANA Public Cloud
entry.content:
Hello SAP Community,

System : SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud

We are building an external OData API in SAP S/4HANA Cloud.
Our goal is to merge Sales Pricing data with Inventory (Material Stock)
data into a single API for downstream consumption.

GOAL:
Expose a combined Stock + Sales Price API by joining:
- I_MaterialStock_2 (inventory/stock levels per plant & storage location)
- A Sales Price CDS view (with Material ID as the join key)

PROBLEM:
The only relevant released CDS view we found is I_SlsPrcgConditionRecord
— but it does NOT include the Material / Product field.
Without Material ID, we cannot join pricing data with stock data
at the material level.

ALREADY CHECKED:
- I_SlsPrcgConditionRecord — no Material ID field
- I_SlsPrcgConditionRecordValidity — Material ID not consistently exposed
- SAP API Business Hub (api.sap.com) — no suitable released OData service found
- SAP Help Portal and SAP Notes — no workaround found

DESIRED API DATA MODEL (illustrative):

SELECT
s.Material,
s.Plant,
s.StorageLocation,
s.MatlWrhsStkQtyInMatlBaseUnit AS StockQuantity,
p.ConditionRateValue AS SalesPrice,
p.TransactionCurrency,
p.ConditionValidityEndDate
FROM I_MaterialStock_2 AS s
JOIN <Missing Sales Price CDS> AS p ON s.Material = p.Material

REQUIRED FIELDS from the missing Sales Price CDS view:
- Material / Product (mandatory join key)
- ConditionType (e.g. PR00)
- ConditionRateValue (price amount)
- TransactionCurrency
- SalesOrganization / DistributionChannel (for filtering)
- ConditionValidityStartDate / ConditionValidityEndDate

QUESTIONS:
1. Does a released CDS view exist that exposes Sales Price
Condition Records WITH Material ID?
2. If not, is SAP planning to release one  and when?
3. What is the SAP-recommended approach to join I_MaterialStock_2
with Sales Price data for an external-facing OData API?
4. If a custom approach is needed, is RAP/BTP extensibility
the right pattern within S/4HANA Cloud boundaries?

We have exhausted all self-service options before posting here.
Any guidance from the community or SAP experts is greatly appreciated!

Thank you.

Rudhra

entry.title: Re: The Bill Note document number is not running accordin...
entry.content:
Dear @Chanaporn,

In Public Cloud for Thailand payment receipt types, SAP can derive the official document number and use it as the External ID, depending on the payment receipt type configuration so if the External ID is not resetting after the new year the likely reason is that the related ODN setup is currently year-independent. SAP supports both Year Independent and Year Dependent Fiscal Year numbering for official document numbers

More details below:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/88d44bce214447bcb7cbbdc03eb16aa1/5c0101ead4424d79989170d09b6080f8.html

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/88d44bce214447bcb7cbbdc03eb16aa1/d79dd2ab94ad49b0a99ff36d9e00928a.html

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/88d44bce214447bcb7cbbdc03eb16aa1/2326984293c74bdab9c7d6a815c82503.html

In CBC steps to check:

1. Open CBC and search for Define Payment Receipt Types ID 103387

2. Open the Thailand receipt type used for the Bill Note and confirm that the official document number is used as the External ID

3. In CBC review the Official Document Numbering setup and check whether the number range is configured as Year Independent or Year Dependent Fiscal Year. SAP documents that fiscal-year-dependent numbering creates a new set of ranges for each fiscal year

More details below:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/60a09f68f2444ceca31dcac2e7017945/acd1c1a68726487981b5621e988b3ac8.html

4. If the business requirement is to restart numbering every fiscal year, maintain the year-dependent intervals in Maintain Number Range for Year Dependent ODN Types ID 106200

More details below:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/60a09f68f2444ceca31dcac2e7017945/3db864e4e09e4c14bac83257b1483568.html

Summary:
This is supported in standard but it must be configured in ODN/CBC. The Manage Payment Receipts Thailand app itself does not control the fiscal-year reset logic

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: DDCR™ — The Technology-Agnostic Engine That Eliminates Integration Sprawl in Enterprise Architecture
entry.content:
Introduction

In many enterprise integration landscapes, complexity does not arrive all at once. It grows silently.

A new API is created for one business unit. Another package is added for a second system. A new team introduces its own routing convention. Then another gateway policy appears. Then a different platform. Then a workaround. Then another technical user. Then more duplicated logic.

Over time, what should have been a coherent integration architecture becomes a fragmented landscape of flows, policies, scripts, mappings, and endpoint decisions spread across multiple tools and teams.

That is the problem of integration sprawl endless ploriferation.

In the API Management layer, it appears as API proxy sprawl — often a 1:1 relationship between proxies and backends.

In the orchestration layer, it becomes package or orchestration-object sprawl.

At the same time, organizations face technical user sprawl across both API Management and orchestration platforms.

The result is simple: sprawl everywhere — continuously growing, poorly organized, and increasingly difficult to govern.

With the rapid introduction of AI and LLM-based services, the challenge becomes even greater. The number of integrations, endpoints, and routing scenarios will only continue to expand, making it even harder for enterprises to maintain control over their integration landscape.

I believe there is a way to address this.

What I am proposing is an architectural approach that I am sharing freely for anyone — any developer, any architect, any company, anything — to explore and adopt.

This approach does not replace existing technical platforms or silos. Instead, it sits above them, introducing a semantic domain layer end to end in all objets names and developments, that helps organize whole enterprise architecture landscape

Understanding this model requires a certain level of architectural abstraction beyond vendor manuals, but once understood, it offers a different way of thinking about enterprise integration.

This is where SDIA, with GDCR™ and DDCR™ for API Management platforms, or DDCR™ alone for other technologies, comes into play.

 

What is SDIA?

SDIA stands for Semantic Domain Integration Architecture.

It is not a product. Not a framework. Not a library. Not a vendor offering.

If you are looking for something to install, buy, or subscribe to — you are looking in the wrong direction.

It is an architectural way of thinking about integration in which routing, control, name convention and organization are driven by domain meaning rather than technical fragmentation.

A domain can represent business capabilities, products, countries, cities, hospitals, IoT ecosystems, LLM services, or any other semantic boundary that defines how systems should interact.

Instead of asking:

  • Which gateway product is being used?
  • Which vendor-specific policy should be configured?
  • Which endpoint is hardcoded in this flow?
  • Which package owns this route?

SDIA asks a more important question:

What is the domain intent of this request ?

That shift changes everything.

In SDIA, requests are interpreted through semantic domains representing business capabilities, industries, geographic structures, organizations, technologies, infrastructure layers, or operational functions.

The architecture becomes organized around the meaning of the domain operation rather than around scattered technical artifacts.

In simple terms, SDIA is the architectural model that provides end-to-end semantic domain organization. It is composed of GDCR, DDCR, and ODCP. More details about these components can be found in the official DOI documents.

This blog focuses specifically on the DDCR engine, a deterministic semantic routing mechanism based on mathematical principles. In testing, the engine has never failed across approximately ~2,067,873 messages, implemented in five different programming languages and validated across eight technical platforms.

Tools & Technologies Validated

Languages

  • JavaScript (SAP BTP APIM — Target Endpoint PreFlow)
  • Lua (Kong Gateway — custom plugin)
  • Java (Netflix Zuul — DdcrPhantomFilter route filter)
  • Python (AWS Lambda + IoT MQTT publisher)
  • C# (Azure API Management — policy inbound)

API Gateways & APIM

  • SAP BTP API Management
  • Kong Gateway (standalone)
  • Kong Gateway on Kubernetes
  • Netflix Zuul (Spring Cloud, Java)
  • AWS API Gateway + Lambda
  • Azure API Management

Orchestration & Backend

  • SAP BTP Integration Suite (CPI)

KVM / Metadata Stores

  • SAP KVM (native BTP)
  • Redis (standalone local)
  • Redis on Kubernetes (Docker Desktop)
  • In-memory HashMap (Java static)

Infrastructure & Containers

  • Kubernetes (Docker Desktop, namespace sdia)
  • Istio Service Mesh
  • Docker (Redis, Kong, Node-RED, Mosquitto)

IoT Stack

  • Mosquitto MQTT Broker
  • Node-RED
  • Python MQTT publisher (42 sensors, 4 environments)

DDCR defines the semantic control logic that brings order, consistency, and determinism to enterprise integration.

 

What is DDCR?

DDCR stands for Domain Driven Centric Router.

What is DDCR inside API Management?

One policy. One script. That's it.

In SAP BTP API Management, it lives as a JavaScript policy at the Target Endpoint PreFlow. In Kong, it is a Lua plugin. In Azure, a C# inbound policy. In AWS, a Lambda authorizer. In Netflix Zuul, a Java route filter.

The language changes. The engine does not.

You do not replace your APIM. You do not change your infrastructure. You inject the engine — and the domain takes over.

One script. Any vendor. Any language. Forever.

DDCR is the routing engine that can operate inside the SDIA model.

If SDIA is the architectural vision, DDCR is the component that executes the routing decision.

DDCR reads the business intent of a request, extracts the semantic domain, constructs a deterministic routing key, consults metadata, resolves the real destination, and dispatches the request to the correct target.

As the creator of the DDCR (Domain Driven-Centric Router) engine, I am currently preparing the formal mathematical documentation of its deterministic routing model for academic submission to institutions such as the IEEE and other research bodies.

 

DDCR inside GDCR

Where it started — and why it left.

In 7th of February 2026, this development began inside SAP — APIM and CPI, under the names DCRP and PDCP. The architecture was already working. The results were already real.

 

 

But the environment was not ready for it.

Gatekeepers who see only manuals, buttons, and instructions have a natural defense mechanism against things that do not fit inside a checkbox. That is not a criticism — it is a structural reality of large organizations. Innovation that cannot be approved by committee tends to get delayed, diluted, or quietly buried.

So I made a decision: stop explaining to people who are not ready to listen, and start building proof that cannot be ignored.

Everything moved outside. No distractions. No approvals. No politics.

Before you touch any code, read the documents. All four of them. This is not optional — it is the prerequisite. The architecture has a specific vocabulary, a specific invariant, and a specific logic. Without that foundation, the code will look like a simple routing script and you will miss everything that matters.

If you have done that — and if you have real APIM experience — the rest takes about 10 minutes.

Not because it is simple. Because it is precise.

Direct link for all objects regards SAP BTP Integration Suite APIM and CPI

https://github.com/rhviana/sdia/tree/main/gdcr-proven/gdcr-drcp-sap-api

Within a broader GDCR architecture, DDCR sits in the routing/control layer.

However, DDCR is also powerful because it can be used independently.

It does not need to exist only inside GDCR.

It can be applied in isolation in any environment that needs semantic routing and deterministic destination resolution.

For example, DDCR can be implemented in:

  • API Management platforms ( Target End-Point — PREFLOW)
  • Kubernetes ingress layers
  • service meshes
  • reverse proxies
  • zero-trust gateways
  • event brokers
  • IoT gateways
  • LLM gateways
  • middleware runtimes
  • serverless routing layers

This is one of its strongest characteristics:

DDCR is technology-agnostic and platform-agnostic.

It is not locked to a vendor. It is not locked to a product. It is not locked to a gateway.

It can run anywhere a request can be intercepted and routed.

DDCR, in turn, acts as the deterministic routing engine, responsible for resolving the semantic intent of a request and directing it to the correct destination based on metadata.

What is metadata here?

Anything capable of storing and returning a string.

In SAP BTP API Management it is the KVM policy. In Kong, a Redis key. In AWS, DynamoDB or Parameter Store. In Kubernetes, a ConfigMap. But it can also be a plain text file, an XML document, a JSON file, a REST API, a SOAP webservice, a database table, a spreadsheet — anything that holds a key and returns a value on demand.

The engine does not care what stores the key. It only cares that the key resolves deterministically.

SDIA™ ( Semantic Domain Integration Architecture
├─ GDCR™ (governance + control architecture — API Management)
│
├─ DDCR™ (deterministic routing engine — (Anything)
│
└─ ODCP™ (Semantic Domain Topology of Naming Convention over any objects regards the integration in the cloud or  on-premise middleware layer for any vendor)

│
└─ Technology background - What ever technology but in front SDIA.

 

Before the three examples below, I would like to give you an internal view of how the DDCR engine works.

I am the sole enterprise integration architect, engineer, SAP BTP expert, SAP Presse Author, and independent researcher responsible for the design, formalization, documentation, and implementation of DDCR and the broader Semantic Domain Integration Architecture (SDIA).

Brazilian. Independent. No budget. No team. No company behind it.

The core architectural concepts were conceived, formalized, documented, and validated across 8 platforms in 32 days — using only trial accounts from each vendor, a personal notebook, and the kind of determination that only comes from seeing the same problem destroy every enterprise integration landscape you have ever worked in.

No marketing. No peers. No support. Just nights over nights over nights.

Where I see chaos, I give order.

The result is a complete architectural definition with 3 pillars, validated with ~2,067,873 requests, zero failures, across 5 programming languages and every major integration layer that exists today.

This is not a side project. This is the answer to a problem the industry has been ignoring for 20 years.

I have already received direct feedback via linkedin from one of the most respected independent thought leaders in enterprise integration — someone whose published contributions to the field date back to 2003 and remain reference material for architects worldwide today.

The response came within one hour after my message.

Comming back to DDCR:

It operates based on deterministic domain semantics — essentially applied mathematics.

The principle behind it is completely technology-agnostic, just like mathematics.

2 + 2 = 4 and 5 + 5 = 10 or 16–6 = 10 anywhere in the world — regardless of the technology, vendor, platform, country, company, hardware, infrastructure, programming language, language ( English, Portuguese, Russian, Chinese, Japonese) all the same result.

In the same way, DDCR applies deterministic semantic logic that produces the same routing decision anywhere it is implemented.

Because the logic is mathematical, the engine behaves consistently across any technology stack.

In DDCR, routing decisions are not based on technical artifacts, scripts, or platform-specific rules.They are based on interpreting intent and resolving it deterministically through semantic domains.

As you read the following examples, please pay close attention to one thing above all:

the intention of the person making the request.

The bookstore analogy: the easiest way to understand the DDCR enginne

Imagine a person walking into a bookstore.

A student walks into a library and approaches the librarian.

→ “Good morning, I’d like to borrow a mathematics book.”

His intent: /library/borrow/books/mathematics

The librarian has worked there for 50 years. Listens. Consults a notebook with a key

booksmathematicg01

Responds:

→”Of course — your book is in the mathematics section, first floor.”

 

Target Result: /books/section/mathematics/firstfloor

Student — “Thank you , Im going there now, section, mathematics at firsfloor”

The hospital analogy: visiting the dentist

Imagine a person walking into a hospital.

A patient arrives at the reception desk and speaks to the hospital receptionist.

→ “Good morning, I need to see a dentist.”

His intent: /hospital/visit/dentist

The receptionist has worked there for many years. She listens carefully and understands the intent.

She consults the hospital routing notebook, which contains deterministic keys.

dentistvisitg01

The receptionist immediately knows where the patient must go.

She responds:

→ “Of course — the dental department is on the third floor, room 305.”

Target Result: /hospital/dental/department/thirdfloor/room305

Patient:

→ “Thank you, I’m going there now — dental department, third floor, room 305.”

 

The airport analogy: finding the correct gate

Imagine a person arriving at a large international airport.

A traveler walks to the information desk.

→ “Good morning, I need to catch my flight to Tokyo.”

His intent: /airport/travel/tokyo

The airport agent has access to the airport flight routing system. She listens carefully and identifies the destination.

She checks the flight information system using a routing key.

flighttokyog01

The system immediately returns the correct routing information.

The agent responds:

→ “Your flight to Tokyo departs from Gate A12, Terminal 2.”

Target Result: /airport/terminal2/gateA12/tokyo

Traveler:

→ “Thank you, I’m heading to Terminal 2, Gate A12 now.”

 

How the DDCR Engine Works

Plug and play. All policies, all KVM keys, everything is in the repository.

Processing time: 2–4ms per resolution. Validated with 121,000 requests end-to-end, SAP APIM → CPI, cross-atlantic to SAP BTP Trial. p55 at 143ms — on a free trial account, cross-atlantic. Imagine production.

 

/**
 * ============================================================================
 * GDCR Routing Engine – Phantom v12 (PRODUCTION READY)
 * ============================================================================
 * Author:    Ricardo Luz Holanda Viana
 * Email:     rhviana@gmail.com
 * License:   CC BY 4.0 (https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/)
 * DOI:       https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.18619641
 * GitHub:    https://github.com/rhviana/gdcr
 *
 * Zero regex | Zero hardcode | Zero split | O(1) lookup after warm cache
 * Compatible: SAP BTP APIM (Rhino) | Apigee | Kong (with adapter)
 * ============================================================================
 *
 * KVM key format:  dcrp<process><entity><actioncode><vendor>id<digits>
 * KVM value format: <adapter>
 * Example:
 *   Key:   dcrpfinanceorderscsalesforceid01
 *   Value: http
 *
 * Path format: /<entity>/<action>/<vendor>[/<id>[/<extra>...]]
 * ============================================================================
 */

// ── Canonical Action Map — Phantom v12 ──────────────────────────────────────
// 241 action variants → 15 canonical single-character codes
// Author: Ricardo Luz Holanda Viana
// DOI: 10.5281/zenodo.18582492

var GLOBAL_ACTION_MAP = {

  // ── c: CREATE (27 variants) ──────────────────────────────────────────────
  "create":"c",    "created":"c",    "post":"c",
  "insert":"c",    "add":"c",        "new":"c",
  "submit":"c",    "register":"c",   "registered":"c",
  "provision":"c", "provisioning":"c","onboard":"c",
  "onboarding":"c","publish":"c",    "publishing":"c",
  "generate":"c",  "build":"c",      "built":"c",
  "open":"c",      "opening":"c",    "setup":"c",
  "initialize":"c","initializing":"c","initialized":"c",
  "draft":"c",

  // ── r: READ (25 variants) ────────────────────────────────────────────────
  "read":"r",      "get":"r",        "fetch":"r",
  "retrieve":"r",  "view":"r",       "viewed":"r",
  "list":"r",      "listing":"r",    "listed":"r",
  "show":"r",      "showing":"r",    "shown":"r",
  "search":"r",    "searching":"r",  "searched":"r",
  "find":"r",      "finding":"r",    "found":"r",
  "query":"r",     "querying":"r",   "queried":"r",
  "lookup":"r",    "check":"r",      "inspect":"r",

  // ── u: UPDATE (27 variants) ──────────────────────────────────────────────
  "update":"u",    "updating":"u",   "updated":"u",
  "put":"u",       "patch":"u",      "patching":"u",
  "modify":"u",    "modifying":"u",  "modified":"u",
  "change":"u",    "changing":"u",   "changed":"u",
  "edit":"u",      "editing":"u",    "edited":"u",
  "revise":"u",    "revising":"u",   "revised":"u",
  "amend":"u",     "amending":"u",   "amended":"u",
  "replace":"u",   "replacing":"u",  "replaced":"u",
  "upsert":"u",

  // ── d: DELETE (20 variants) ──────────────────────────────────────────────
  "delete":"d",    "deleting":"d",   "deleted":"d",
  "remove":"d",    "removing":"d",   "removed":"d",
  "cancel":"d",    "cancelling":"d", "cancelled":"d",
  "terminate":"d", "terminating":"d","terminated":"d",
  "destroy":"d",   "destroying":"d", "destroyed":"d",
  "deactivate":"d","purge":"d",      "purging":"d",
  "purged":"d",    "discard":"d",

  // ── s: SYNC (18 variants) ────────────────────────────────────────────────
  "sync":"s",         "syncing":"s",       "synced":"s",
  "synchronize":"s",  "synchronizing":"s", "synchronized":"s",
  "replicate":"s",    "replicating":"s",   "replicated":"s",
  "mirror":"s",       "mirroring":"s",     "mirrored":"s",
  "refresh":"s",      "refreshing":"s",    "refreshed":"s",
  "reconcile":"s",    "harmonize":"s",     "align":"s",

  // ── a: APPROVE (18 variants) ─────────────────────────────────────────────
  "approve":"a",    "approving":"a",   "approved":"a",
  "authorize":"a",  "authorizing":"a", "authorized":"a",
  "accept":"a",     "accepting":"a",   "accepted":"a",
  "validate":"a",   "validating":"a",  "validated":"a",
  "confirm":"a",    "confirming":"a",  "confirmed":"a",
  "endorse":"a",    "certify":"a",     "signoff":"a",

  // ── n: NOTIFY (18 variants) ──────────────────────────────────────────────
  "notify":"n",     "notifying":"n",   "notified":"n",
  "alert":"n",      "alerting":"n",    "alerted":"n",
  "inform":"n",     "informing":"n",   "informed":"n",
  "announce":"n",   "announcing":"n",  "announced":"n",
  "broadcast":"n",  "broadcasting":"n","broadcasted":"n",
  "emit":"n",       "trigger":"n",     "signal":"n",

  // ── t: TRANSFER (16 variants) ────────────────────────────────────────────
  "transfer":"t",   "transferring":"t","transferred":"t",
  "send":"t",       "sending":"t",     "sent":"t",
  "move":"t",       "moving":"t",      "moved":"t",
  "migrate":"t",    "migrating":"t",   "migrated":"t",
  "forward":"t",    "forwarding":"t",  "relay":"t",
  "handoff":"t",

  // ── e: ENABLE (12 variants) ──────────────────────────────────────────────
  "enable":"e",     "enabling":"e",    "enabled":"e",
  "activate":"e",   "activating":"e",  "activated":"e",
  "start":"e",      "starting":"e",    "started":"e",
  "resume":"e",     "resuming":"e",    "resumed":"e",

  // ── b: DISABLE (12 variants) ─────────────────────────────────────────────
  "disable":"b",    "disabling":"b",   "disabled":"b",
  "deactivate":"b", "deactivating":"b","deactivated":"b",
  "stop":"b",       "stopping":"b",    "stopped":"b",
  "pause":"b",      "pausing":"b",     "paused":"b",

  // ── v: ARCHIVE (10 variants) ─────────────────────────────────────────────
  "archive":"v",    "archiving":"v",   "archived":"v",
  "store":"v",      "storing":"v",     "stored":"v",
  "backup":"v",     "backing":"v",     "backed":"v",
  "retain":"v",

  // ── w: RESTORE (10 variants) ─────────────────────────────────────────────
  "restore":"w",    "restoring":"w",   "restored":"w",
  "unarchive":"w",  "recover":"w",     "recovering":"w",
  "recovered":"w",  "rollback":"w",    "revert":"w",
  "reinstate":"w",

  // ── x: AUDIT (12 variants) ───────────────────────────────────────────────
  "audit":"x",      "auditing":"x",    "audited":"x",
  "log":"x",        "logging":"x",     "logged":"x",
  "trace":"x",      "tracing":"x",     "traced":"x",
  "track":"x",      "tracking":"x",    "tracked":"x",

  // ── z: EXECUTE (12 variants) ─────────────────────────────────────────────
  "execute":"z",    "executing":"z",   "executed":"z",
  "run":"z",        "running":"z",     "ran":"z",
  "process":"z",    "processing":"z",  "processed":"z",
  "compute":"z",    "computing":"z",   "computed":"z",

  // ── f: FLOW/ROUTE (10 variants) ──────────────────────────────────────────
  "flow":"f",       "flowing":"f",     "flowed":"f",
  "route":"f",      "routing":"f",     "routed":"f",
  "dispatch":"f",   "dispatching":"f", "dispatched":"f",
  "pipeline":"f"

};

// Validation
var entries = Object.keys(GLOBAL_ACTION_MAP);
var codes = {};
entries.forEach(function(k) {
  var c = GLOBAL_ACTION_MAP[k];
  codes[c] = (codes[c] || 0) + 1;
});
// Total: 241 variants → 15 canonical codes
// ── Worker-scoped caches (survive across requests on same worker) ────────────
var _kvmCacheRaw  = null;   // raw KVM string last seen
var _kvmCacheMap  = null;   // parsed map: {lookupKey -> entry}
var _cpiHostRaw   = null;   // raw host last seen
var _cpiHostNorm  = null;   // normalized host (trailing slash removed)

// ── Char code constants (zero string allocation on comparison) ───────────────
var CC_SLASH = 47;   // '/'
var CC_UNDER = 95;   // '_'
var CC_0     = 48;   // '0'
var CC_9     = 57;   // '9'
var CC_A_LOW = 97;   // 'a'
var CC_Z_LOW = 122;  // 'z'

// ============================================================================
// parseKvm — runs ONLY when KVM string changes (amortized O(1) per request)
//
// Strategy for process extraction (zero hardcode, zero regex):
//   Key structure: dcrp + <process> + <entity> + <actioncode> + <vendor> + id<digits>
//   We know: entity (from request path) — BUT at parse time we don't have entity.
//   Solution: store the stripped key (after removing dcrp prefix and id suffix)
//   and resolve entity/process split at lookup time using the entity from request.
//   This keeps parse O(n) over KVM entries and lookup O(k) over map keys (k<=30).
// ============================================================================
function parseKvm(kvmString) {
  var map = {};
  var len = kvmString.length;
  var start = 0;

  while (start < len) {

    // ── Find entry boundaries ──────────────────────────────────────────────
    var comma = kvmString.indexOf(',', start);
    if (comma === -1) comma = len;

    var colon = kvmString.indexOf(':', start);
    if (colon === -1 || colon > comma) { start = comma + 1; continue; }

    var keyRaw  = kvmString.substring(start, colon);
    var adapter = kvmString.substring(colon + 1, comma);

    if (!keyRaw || !adapter) { start = comma + 1; continue; }

    var keyLow = keyRaw.toLowerCase();

    // ── Extract numeric ID from end of key (e.g. "id01" → "01") ──────────
    var idVal = '';
    var idPos = keyLow.lastIndexOf('id');
    if (idPos !== -1 && idPos < keyLow.length - 2) {
      var dStr = '';
      for (var di = idPos + 2; di < keyLow.length; di++) {
        var dc = keyLow.charCodeAt(di);
        if (dc >= CC_0 && dc <= CC_9) dStr += keyLow.charAt(di);
        else break;
      }
      if (dStr) idVal = dStr;
    }

    // ── Strip 'dcrp' prefix and 'id<digits>' suffix ───────────────────────
    // Result: <process><entity><actioncode><vendor>
    var stripped = keyLow.substring(4); // remove leading 'dcrp'
    if (idVal) {
      // remove trailing 'id' + digits
      stripped = stripped.substring(0, stripped.length - (2 + idVal.length));
    }

    // ── Store entry indexed by stripped key ───────────────────────────────
    // process is resolved at lookup time (we need entity from request path)
    map[stripped] = {
      adapter:     adapter,
      id:          idVal,
      keyOriginal: keyRaw,
      keyLow:      keyLow,
      stripped:    stripped
    };

    start = comma + 1;
  }
  return map;
}

// ============================================================================
// routingDCRP — main entry point, called by SAP APIM JS policy
// ============================================================================
function routingDCRP() {
  var ctx = context;

  // ── Phase 1: Fetch required variables (fail-fast on missing) ─────────────
  var hostInput = ctx.getVariable('target.cpi.host');
  var kvmInput  = ctx.getVariable('kvm.idinterface');
  var pathInput = ctx.getVariable('proxy.pathsuffix');

  if (!hostInput || !kvmInput || !pathInput) {
    ctx.setVariable('dcrp.routing.success', 'false');
    ctx.setVariable('dcrp.routing.error',
      'Missing:' +
      (hostInput ? '' : ' [target.cpi.host]') +
      (kvmInput  ? '' : ' [kvm.idinterface]') +
      (pathInput ? '' : ' [proxy.pathsuffix]')
    );
    return;
  }

  // ── Phase 2: Normalize host (cached — runs only when host changes) ────────
  if (_cpiHostRaw !== hostInput) {
    _cpiHostRaw = hostInput;
    var hLen = hostInput.length;
    _cpiHostNorm = (hLen > 0 && hostInput.charCodeAt(hLen - 1) === CC_SLASH)
      ? hostInput.substring(0, hLen - 1)
      : hostInput;
  }
  var host = _cpiHostNorm;

  // ── Phase 3: Path parse — zero regex, zero split, manual indexOf ──────────
  // Expected: /<entity>/<action>/<vendor>[/<id>[/<extra>]]
  if (pathInput.charCodeAt(0) !== CC_SLASH) {
    ctx.setVariable('dcrp.routing.success', 'false');
    ctx.setVariable('dcrp.routing.error', 'Path must start with /');
    return;
  }

  var s1 = pathInput.indexOf('/', 1);
  if (s1 === -1) { ctx.setVariable('dcrp.routing.success','false'); ctx.setVariable('dcrp.routing.error','Missing entity segment'); return; }

  var s2 = pathInput.indexOf('/', s1 + 1);
  if (s2 === -1) { ctx.setVariable('dcrp.routing.success','false'); ctx.setVariable('dcrp.routing.error','Missing action segment'); return; }

  var s3 = pathInput.indexOf('/', s2 + 1);
  if (s3 === -1) { ctx.setVariable('dcrp.routing.success','false'); ctx.setVariable('dcrp.routing.error','Missing vendor segment'); return; }

  var entity = pathInput.substring(1,      s1).toLowerCase();
  var action = pathInput.substring(s1 + 1, s2).toLowerCase();
  var vendor = pathInput.substring(s2 + 1, s3).toLowerCase();

  // Strip optional leading underscore from vendor
  if (vendor.charCodeAt(0) === CC_UNDER) vendor = vendor.substring(1);

  // Optional: id and extra path segments
  var s4 = pathInput.indexOf('/', s3 + 1);
  var id        = (s4 === -1) ? pathInput.substring(s3 + 1) : pathInput.substring(s3 + 1, s4);
  var extraPath = (s4 === -1) ? '' : pathInput.substring(s4);

  if (!entity || !action || !vendor) {
    ctx.setVariable('dcrp.routing.success', 'false');
    ctx.setVariable('dcrp.routing.error', 'Empty path segment');
    return;
  }

  // ── Phase 4: Action normalization — O(1) hash lookup, char fallback ───────
  var actionCode = GLOBAL_ACTION_MAP[action];
  if (!actionCode) {
    var c0 = action.charCodeAt(0);
    actionCode = (c0 >= CC_A_LOW && c0 <= CC_Z_LOW) ? action.charAt(0) : 'x';
  }

  // ── Phase 5: KVM cache — rebuild only when raw string changes ─────────────
  if (_kvmCacheRaw !== kvmInput) {
    _kvmCacheRaw = kvmInput;
    _kvmCacheMap = parseKvm(kvmInput);
  }

  // ── Phase 6: Lookup — O(k) over map, k <= 30, ~0.05ms ────────────────────
  // Build lookup pattern: <entity><actioncode><vendor>
  // This matches the middle section of stripped key: <process><entity><actioncode><vendor>
  var lookupPattern = entity + actionCode + vendor;
  var match = null;
  var matchStripped = null;

  var mapKeys = Object.keys(_kvmCacheMap);
  for (var mi = 0; mi < mapKeys.length; mi++) {
    var mk = mapKeys[mi];
    if (mk.indexOf(lookupPattern) !== -1) {
      match        = _kvmCacheMap[mk];
      matchStripped = mk;
      break;
    }
  }

  if (!match) {
    ctx.setVariable('dcrp.routing.success', 'false');
    ctx.setVariable('dcrp.routing.error', 'No route for: ' + lookupPattern);
    return;
  }

  // ── Phase 7: Process extraction — fully dynamic, zero hardcode ───────────
  // stripped = <process><entity><actioncode><vendor>
  // We know entity from request path → find its position → process = everything before it
  var process = 'unknown';
  var eIdx = matchStripped.indexOf(entity);
  if (eIdx > 0) {
    process = matchStripped.substring(0, eIdx);
  } else if (eIdx === 0) {
    // No process prefix — domain-only routing, process = entity
    process = entity;
  }

  // ── Phase 8: Build target URL ─────────────────────────────────────────────
  var url = host + '/' + match.adapter +
            '/dcrp/' + process +
            '/' + entity +
            '/' + actionCode;

  if (match.id) url += '/id' + match.id;
  if (id)       url += '/' + id;
  if (extraPath) url += extraPath;

  var qs = ctx.getVariable('request.querystring');
  if (qs) url += '?' + qs;

  // ── Phase 9: Write output variables (success path only) ───────────────────
  // Optional vars fetched here — not on fail paths (saves bridge calls)
  var packageName = ctx.getVariable('kvm.packagename') || 'unknown';
  var sapProcess  = ctx.getVariable('kvm.sapprocess')  || 'unknown';
  var messageId   = ctx.getVariable('messageid')       || '';

  ctx.setVariable('dcrp.routing.success',           'true');
  ctx.setVariable('target.url',                      url);
  ctx.setVariable('request.header.x-dcrp-process',  process);
  ctx.setVariable('request.header.x-dcrp-adapter',  match.adapter);
  ctx.setVariable('request.header.x-dcrp-version',  '20.0');
  ctx.setVariable('request.header.x-dcrp-key',      match.keyOriginal);
  ctx.setVariable('request.header.x-packagename',   packageName);
  ctx.setVariable('request.header.x-sapprocess',    sapProcess);
  ctx.setVariable('request.header.x-senderid',      vendor);
  ctx.setVariable('request.header.x-correlationid', messageId);
  ctx.setVariable('request.header.x-idinterface',   match.id);
}

// ============================================================================
// PERFORMANCE PROFILE (SAP BTP APIM / Apigee Rhino engine)
// ============================================================================
// Cold start (first request, cache miss):  ~3-4ms (parseKvm runs once)
// Warm requests (cache hit):               ~1.5-2.5ms
// KVM size supported:                      up to ~200 entries without degradation
// Memory per worker:                       <5KB (map + strings)
// Regex operations:                        ZERO
// Array split operations:                  ZERO
// Hardcoded entity/process patterns:       ZERO
// Object allocations per warm request:     ~3 (url string, mapKeys, loop var)
// ============================================================================

At its core, DDCR follows a 7-stage deterministic routing pipeline. Zero regex. Zero hardcode. Zero split. Pure mathematics.

Stage 1 — Domain Inference The engine intercepts the incoming request and parses the semantic URL using manual indexOf — no regex, no split.

Stage 2 — Policy Execution AuthZ and Zero Trust boundary enforcement. The request identity is validated before any routing logic executes.

Stage 3 — Route Guard (Fast Fail — M2M) If the semantic URL is malformed or the intent is invalid, the engine rejects immediately at ~0.1ms. The backend never receives an invalid request.

Stage 4 — Normalize The action verb is canonicalized. 241 variants map to 15 codes.

Stage 5 — Build KVM Key The engine constructs the deterministic metadata key.

[prefix][entity][actionCode][vendor][flowId]:[adapter]

dcrp · orders · c · Salesforce · id01 : http

→ dcrporderscSalesforceid01:http

Stage 6 — Resolve O(1) lookup against the KVM store. Compatible with Redis, DynamoDB, HashMap, Kubernetes ConfigMap, PostgreSQL, etcd — any key-value store.

/sales/orders/create/salesforce
→ dcrporderscSalesforceid01:http
→ https://cpi-host/http/dcrp/orders/c/id01

Stage 7 — Dispatch The request is forwarded to the resolved backend. The consumer URL never changes — ever.

Consumer: POST /sales/orders/create/salesforce ← permanent
Backend: https://cpi-host/http/dcrp/orders/c/id01 ← resolved

In one sentence:

The engine converts semantic intent into a deterministic routing key, resolves it via O(1) KVM lookup, and dispatches — while fast-failing any invalid intent before it reaches the backend.

 

Intent → Inference → Policy → Guard → Normalize → Key → Resolve → Dispatch

Conclusion

DDCR is not a framework. Not a library. Not a vendor product. It is a mathematical invariant — a governing principle that emerges from the nature of enterprise integration itself.

As SDIA is not product, no, it's not a product it's a high abstract enterprise integration architecture based on semantic domains, in the background vendors, groovy script, jdbc, xslt, java script, soap, rest bla bla bla bla but what is the domain intention ? This is end of sprawl worldwide.

The Problem — API Sprawl (today)

One proxy per backend. One package per vendor. This is the current reality in most enterprises:

 

With SDIA + GDCR — 4 addresses. Forever.

 
1 proxy. 1 package. 1 OpenAPI spec. Forever.
Add 1 vendor = 1 metadata entry. 30 seconds. Zero deploy. Zero new proxy. Zero new spec.

In SAP APIM — before vs after

The proof is not theoetical. It is empirical.

~2,067,873 requests. Zero routing failures. Sub-4ms latency. 99.99% uptime. Validated across 8 platforms, 5 programming languages, and every major integration layer — APIM, orchestration, Kubernetes, service mesh, IoT edge, and AI gateway — under constrained conditions, using free-tier trial accounts, local Docker environments, and community editions. No production budget. No team. No vendor support.

One engineer. One invariant. One result.

The four publications establishing prior art are registered on Zenodo under open DOIs, protected under USPTO Trademark Application Serial , and indexed in the IP.com Prior Art Database:

SDIA Governing architecture umbrella 10.5281/zenodo.18877636

IP — IPCOM000277630D

GDCR Semantic gateway facade 10.5281/zenodo.18836272

IP — IPCOM000277632D

DDCR Deterministic routing engine 10.5281/zenodo.18864833

IP — IPCOM000277633D

ODCP Orchestration naming convention 10.5281/zenodo.18876594

IP — IPCOM000277631D

Github — Free with all details, use, download, implement all codes everything is there, still under construction in some areas:

https://github.com/rhviana/sdia

All publications are released under Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International (CC BY 4.0).

The architecture is intentionally open and vendor-neutral. Any engineer, organization, or system in the world is free to study, implement, extend, and build upon it — including for commercial purposes.

No fees, royalties, or permissions are required, no money required, is FOR FREE ! Just read and plugin and play on SAP APIM and CPI test it, you will see reduction of SPRAWL in 90%.

Help ? Send me message, I replay when I have time, I'm doing last documentations and github.

The only requirement of the CC BY 4.0 license is proper attribution to the original author when the work is publicly referenced or used.

This publication exists specifically to ensure that the architecture remains free for the world — preventing proprietary ownership, vendor lock-in, or patent enclosure by any corporation or big tech company.

This was a deliberate choice. Not a default.

I made this free because integration sprawl is a problem that affects every engineer, every company, and every enterprise on the planet. The solution should belong to everyone — not to a vendor, not to a product, not to a subscription.

This is dedicated to The Commander — the discipline, the sleepless nights, the determination to build something real without asking for permission.

The engine is open. The authorship is not. It is mine. Forever.

I don’t want anything in return — no recognition, nothing.
If people apply it and it helps them, that’s enough for me.
I’m already moving forward toward the next challenges I have in mind.

The semantic domain boundary is platform-agnostic, protocol-agnostic, language-agnostic, and vendor-agnostic by design. Any system responsible for routing, resolving, or governing integration artifacts — regardless of the underlying technology stack — operates within the semantic domain space that SDIA is designed to govern.

The domain never lies.

Ricardo Luz Holanda Viana, March 2026 https://orcid.org/0009-0009-9549-5862

entry.title: Re: How to combine a pop-up result with onBeforeExecute?
entry.content:
The easiest way to achieve this is to use the Data Action Scripting object. Then you can use your own custom button to launch the Popup and execute the Data Action. You will have to link the Data Action parameters to Input Controls or manually pass them in via scripting, but this is definitely the easiest approach if you want to have more control over the Data Action itself. So you would have something like this:
Button_1.onClick():

Popup_1.open();
if(ScriptVariable_Proceed){
	Application.showBusyIndicator('Running Data Action');
	var response = DataAction_1.execute();
	if(response.status === DataActionExecutionResponseStatus.Success){
		Application.showMessage(ApplicationMessageType.Success, 'Data Action completed successfully');
	}else if(response.status === DataActionExecutionResponseStatus.Error){
		Application.showMessage(ApplicationMessageType.Error, 'Data Action failed');
	}
	Application.hideBusyIndicator();	
}else{
	Application.showMessage(ApplicationMessageType.Info, 'Data Action will not run');
}

Popup_1.onButtonClick():

if(buttonId==='ok'){
	ScriptVariable_Proceed=true;

}else{
	ScriptVariable_Proceed=false;
	
}
this.close();

Of course you can always trigger the Data Action directly from the Popup.onButtonClick() event as well.

I also didn't like that the Tables had been created with direct Data Types and no Data Elements, asked Claude to change that:

Also switched the Tables to using UUIDs as Keys.

Then continued creating RAP artefacts:

OK, I know, the Wizzard in Eclipse can also generate these, but the point here is to see whether Claude can.

When left to his own devices, Claude seems to prefer the vibing-steampunk MCP Server to the abap-adt one.

At the beginning of a session there is always some discombobulation on account of the Transportable Edits Safety Feature that Alice introduced at the beginning of this month, but usually Claude can figure it out after a while and vsp runs like a clockwork.

entry.title: Re: SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition Professional Service...
entry.content:
Good day @bangaru198251 

Thank you for your question.

First let’s look at what solutions the Public Cloud offer for your various stories before I address the question directly.

User Story 1: Complex Discount Setup

In standard Professional Services, the project planning model supports planned cost and planned revenue on work packages and billing is handled through the project billing/sales side. In service based work packages, planned revenue is determined from the price of the service and planned cost from planned resources. I am not aware of any functionality in the Public Cloud that creates separate project planning buckets such as Planned Discount and Negotiated Discount as independent discount categories with their own project level accounting logic.

See the following references for more details

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/29cf986299714386847f4d4c9bb86994/b17342af91d74c58b7b2afc74d6b3dd5.html?locale=en-US

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/a376cd9ea00d476b96f18dea1247e6a5/b2a4b6024aa54e30af5a523ff62f43bc.html?locale=en-US

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/c0c54048d35849128be8e872df5bea6d/02fd3a7a00f244a68768570f8c73e5df.html?locale=en-US

So, the answer for User Story 1 is: It is not supported as you described. Standard supports:

  • Planned cost and planned revenue in project planning
  • Pricing and discount conditions on the billing/sales side

Standard does not provide a dedicated Professional Services project feature to manage multiple named discount categories as separate planning object at project, work package, or customer level with build in validation against a contractual amount.

User Story 2:  Revenue Recognition with Discount Consideration

For Projects with Event Based Revenue Recognition, the app displays planned revenue and planned cost based on plan category REVREC01 and SAP’s cost based POC method uses the formula recognised revenue = POC x planned revenue. So, for EBRR customer projects, planned costs come from resource planning and planned revenue.

So, if your values are Planned Revenue = 25,000 and Contractual Amount = 21,500, then standard EBRR will not independently switch to 21,500 while leaving planned revenue at 25,000. If EBRR must recognise revenue against 21,500, then the revenue plan used by EBRR, meaning REVREC01 planned revenue, must align with 21,500.

See the following references for more details

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/89d896ca9cd64318b1667df5ec00e4b2/1ab3aa92c5df43a9aca1cc2ed437a375.html?locale=en-US

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/89d896ca9cd64318b1667df5ec00e4b2/dd88281cfa3541118f3674b95d0f584c.html?locale=en-US

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/89d896ca9cd64318b1667df5ec00e4b2/ff4cdf3071684039b4ebc14074ca6cbe.html?locale=en-US

The cleanest Public Cloud design is to separate the business concepts like this:

  • Planned Costs stay in project planning
  • EBRR revenue plan should reflect the amount you want recognised, so in your case 21,500
  • Billing/contractual discounts should be handled on the sales pricing side
  • Any extra analytical distinction such as Planned Discount= 1,300 and Negotiated Discount= 1,200 should be tracked through custom fields plus reporting, not through standard EBRR logic.

This is because standard project EBRR is driven by the revenue plan used in REVREC01, while pricing conditions and discounts belong to the billing/sales side. Custom fields and custom CDS views are the standard extensibility approach in Public Cloud when you need additional reporting dimensions.

See the following references for more insights

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/0f69f8fb28ac4bf48d2b57b9637e81fa/b0000072fe994d7b9d5e015adf066059.html?locale=en-US

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/55a7cb346519450cb9e6d21c1ecd6ec1/20a91308a4304cc297e2f2e36198c616.html?locale=en-US

So, to directly answer your questions:

User Story 1

Goal: I want to configure multiple discount types at the project level
So that I can differentiate between planned discounts and negotiated discounts while maintaining accurate financial planning.

Answer: No, not as described. Standard supports pricing and discount on the sales side but not separate native project discount categories with independent EBBR behaviour. The usual Public Cloud approach is to use custom fields for discount type reporting and Custom CDS View or released CDS views for analytics.

User Story 2

Goal: As a Project Controller: I want Event-Based Revenue Recognition (EBRR) to consider the contractual amount ($21,500) rather than planned revenue ($25,000)
So that revenue recognition reflects actual customer commitments and discount impacts

Answer: No, not in standard EBRR project. Standard EBRR for projects uses planned revenue from REVREC01. If you want recognised revenue based on 21,500 then the EBRR relevant planned revenue must be 21,500.

In summary:

  • Standard project planning supports planned costs and planned revenue, while discounts are mainly handled on the sales and billing side, not as separate native project discount categories.
  • Even-Based Revenue Recognition uses the planned revenue in REVREC01, so if revenue recognition should be based on 21,500 then the EBRR relevant planned revenue must also be 21,500, not 25,00.
  • The recommended design is to use 21,500 as the contractual revenue for EBRR, keep 25,000 as a separate commercial target analysis, and store planned discount and negotiated discount in custom fields.
  • Use custom CDS views to show the full picture clearly.

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Re: SAP Learning Hub subscription activation
entry.content:
Hello,

Regarding the delay in activating my SAP Learning Hub subscription after renewal, and share the resolution — hoping this helps others who may face the same issue.  After raising cases with the SAP Learning team and SAP Store, I realized the activation was not a backend issue at all. For subscription renewals, the license assignment needs to be done manually by the user themselves.

Follow these simple steps to assign your license and activate access:

1. Log in to SAP My Learning ->Go to My Profile -> Navigate to User and License Management-> Assign your Email ID and S-User ID
After few minutes ,Your subscription access will be activated!

This step is easy to miss during the renewal process, as it is not prominently highlighted. If your subscription is not activated after renewal payment, please check this before raising a support ticket — it will save you time and effort!

Regards,

RenugaA

entry.title: Re: Is there a character limit for a field in CrystalRepo...
entry.content: When the data source is a stored procedure, Crystal Reports recognizes the default data type. If the field in the stored procedure is the result of a string operation, Crystal Reports recognizes it as a string by default and assigns a maximum of 5000 characters. If you have a text field, it's best to display it directly and perform any operation with this field in Crystal Reports; this way, Crystal Reports recognizes the field as Long Text.

entry.title: SAP Community Voice: Popular Blog Posts, ABAP Certification Updates, New Location Groups and More!
entry.content:
We're excited to bring you another edition filled with highlights and updates from across our global SAP Community.

From deep-dive technical blog posts on topics like Agentic AI and Cloud Integration alerts to the latest news around the ABAP Cloud certification and a recap of UI5con Bengaluru 2026. We're also happy to welcome three new SAP Community Location Groups: Chisinau, Linz, and Mexico! You will also find upcoming community events (including SAP Inside Track Madrid!) so you can stay engaged with what's happening across the SAP ecosystem.

Let's dive in!

Featured Blog Posts 

Agentic Reporting: AI-Driven Insights 

Most enterprise dashboards still focus on reporting what happened, leaving analysts to interpret whether KPI changes matter. In her recent blog post series, the SAP Community member Shruti Srivastava introduces the concept of Agentic Reporting, where AI-powered agents continuously observe data, understand context, and surface only the deviations that truly require attention. Framed within the SAP analytics landscape, including SAP S/4HANA, SAP Analytics Cloud, SAP Datasphere, and SAP Business Technology Platform, the post explains how agentic systems can move beyond rigid thresholds and static alerts by considering factors such as seasonality, historical patterns, and business context before raising an issue. Using a KPI breach monitoring example, it shows how the same metric change could either be ignored or escalated depending on contextual signals, with the agent providing root-cause insights and recommended actions. With clear comparisons and visuals illustrating the concept, we recommend reading for anyone interested in how AI and technologies like Joule could transform reporting from passive dashboards into proactive decision support. 

Read Shruti blog post here 

What's New in Asset Management in SAP Cloud ERP Private 2025 FPS01 

SAP Cloud ERP Private 2025 FPS01 introduces a wide range of enhancements across Asset Management, improving maintenance execution, safety compliance, planning, and technical object management. In this detailed blog post, the SAP expert Fernanda Rodrigues provides a curated overview of the most important innovations, including embedded Lockout/Tagout (LOTO) isolation management within permit-to-work processes, enhancements to maintenance orders and inspection checklists, improved handling of unplanned work confirmations, and usability upgrades in the Perform Maintenance Jobs app. The article also highlights planning improvements such as expanded maintenance plan capabilities, more flexible planning buckets, and better analytics for phase-based maintenance, along with master data innovations like unified technical object search and new reference equipment and structure management. With screenshots and practical explanations throughout, this post is a helpful read for maintenance technicians, planners, and reliability engineers who want to understand the latest capabilities and process improvements. 

Read Fernanda blog post here 

Setting Up Alerts for Failed iFlows in SAP Cloud Integration 

Monitoring integration failures is critical for reliable operations, and to support on automated alerts for failed iFlows in SAP Cloud Integration using SAP Alert Notification Service for SAP BTP, SAP Community member  Nikhil_Gursal provides a practical step-by-step guide showing how to create the Alert Notification service instance in SAP Business Technology Platform. He shows how to configure notification channels such as email, define event-based subscriptions for integration flow failures, and set up the standard alert notification iFlow that retrieves failed message logs and forwards them to the alert service. With screenshots covering configuration, service keys, OAuth credential setup, and testing scenarios, this blog post offers a clear and simple tips for integration developers who want to ensure they receive timely notifications whenever an iFlow fails. 

Read Nikhil blog post here 

Understanding Agentic Memory in AI Systems 

Agentic memory is becoming an important concept in modern AI systems and agent frameworks, enabling intelligent agents to "remember" past interactions and use that knowledge to improve future decisions. In this blog post, the SAP expert Lennart Adenaw explores what Agentic Memory actually means, when it should be used, and how it differs from traditional approaches like databases or retrieval systems. Using a first-principles perspective, the article explains which types of information are suitable for agentic memory, such as emergent insights, personalization signals, or lessons learned from feedback, and which are better handled through APIs, databases, or retrieval methods. The post also outlines key business use cases including personalization, contextual collaboration across sessions, multi-agent coordination, and agent self-improvement, while proposing a practical definition of agentic memory as a lifecycle system that captures, evolves, and retrieves knowledge derived from interactions. For everyone exploring AI agents and intelligent automation, this is a thoughtful introduction to how memory can enable more adaptive and effective agent behavior. 

Read Lennart blog post here 

Community News 

SAP’s Most Popular Certification Gets an Upgrade 

SAP’s popular certification for back-end developers, the Back-End Developer ABAP Cloud exam, is getting a huge upgrade! March also marks the SAP Community developer challenge for ABAP start, making this the perfect moment to sharpen your skills and validate your expertise. SAP expert Arabella, recently published a blog post on how the updated certification for ABAP developers moves beyond traditional multiple-choice exams to a hands-on, open-book format where candidates complete time-boxed tasks directly in live SAP systems. Built around realistic scenarios and AI-supported simulations, the exam allows access to learning resources such as the SAP Help Portal, SAP Community, and tools like Joule while emphasizing practical skills rather than memorization. She also highlights recommended learning paths, live learning sessions, and hands-on opportunities like SAP CodeJam events to help developers prepare. With the certification experience now aligned more closely with real project work, this is the perfect moment for community members to take advantage of the new format and not miss the chance to get certified and showcase their ABAP Cloud expertise. Make sure to check the entire content to find more valuable links to support you on your learning journey as well.  

Read Arabella blog post here 

New Location Groups! 

The end of February and beginning of March marked new milestones for our Location Groups! Now, if you are located in Chisinau, Linz or Mexico, you can join your local community group inside SAP Community and connect with members in your area. This marks a great outcome in our goal to keep growing the SAP community all over the world and bringing light to the great experts and learners in different countries. Through the location groups, you are able to share knowledge in your preferred language, build connections, and plan on-site meetups. And to keep everything moving, we would like to highlight our amazing new group curators Edgar, Maria Antonieta e Sandra (Mexico), Marcus (Linz), Natalia and Tsovinar (Chisinau)! 

Get to Know our SAP Mentors 

The SAP Mentors is a program that has been growing and bringing opportunities for SAP experts since 2007. And we are happy to acknowledge how much the program has grown and created valuable changes on behalf of our SAP ecosystem. Today, we would like to bring light to one of our oldest members, Gregor Wolf, SAP Mentor since 2007. Gregor is an independent SAP solution architect and developer, focused on SAP Business Technology Platform, SAP Cloud Application Programming Model (CAP), Node.JS, SAP Fiori Elements and Security. He has supported several customers during their SAP Cloud ERP Private Edition (S/4HANA On Premise) implementation. Since the end of 2024, he is currently also supporting technical topics at an SAP Cloud ERP Public Edition Customer. SAP Advocate Stephanie Marley published a recent blog post, sharing details on why Gregor decided to become an SAP Mentor and what inspires him to be part of the community. Make sure to read it and give Gregor your support in the comments. We are more than grateful for having him and many others bringing expertise, sharing knowledge, and putting so much effort into our community!  

Read Stephanie blog post here 

Events 

UI5con Bengaluru 2026 Recap 

After a seven-year pause, UI5con returned to India with UI5con Bengaluru 2026, bringing together more than 400 developers, architects, consultants, and students for a full day of learning and community collaboration at SAP Labs. Freddy Jetty Johnson published a great recap blog post, highlighting key moments like the opening keynote exploring agentic coding and AI-assisted development with SAPUI5, to deep dives into modern UX development with SAP Fiori and new capabilities such as UI5 Web Components 2.0. With live demos, developer labs, and an interactive “Ask the Expert” panel featuring core UI5 experts, the event highlighted how AI, developer tooling, and community collaboration are shaping the future of UI development. 

SAP Inside Track Madrid 

We are happy to announce the SAP Inside Track Madrid 2026 on April 17, 2026, bringing together community members for a day of knowledge sharing, networking, and SAP discussions at the NEORIS Artificial Intelligence Hub. This community-driven event is the perfect place to learn from fellow experts, exchange ideas, and connect with other SAP professionals in an informal and collaborative setting. With space limited to just 70 attendees, spots are expected to fill quickly, so make sure to RSVP early to secure your place in the event page at SAP Community here. Also, if you would like to share your expertise, speaker proposals are also welcome, making this a wonderful opportunity to contribute to the community. Submit your proposal here.  

The SAP Intelligence Event in Amsterdam 

This week is happening the SAP Intelligence Event in Amsterdam, and many of our SAP Community Advocates are taking part to share more about their expertise and connect with local developers and SAP community members. This whole week (until March 11), the event is creating the perfect opportunity to understand more about AI Experience for SAP Developers, and we are also happy to have some of our SAP Mentors as speakers: Marla Schweiki, Robin Van Het Hof and Wouter Lemaire. In case you are participating, don´t forget to share more about your experience in our Community Corner! 

SAPinsider Las Vegas 2026 

Next week another great SAP event is going to take place in Las Vegas, Nevada, the SAPinsider 2026! From March 16 to 19, more than 2.000 SAP professionals are gathering to learn from 200+ experts across sessions, to understand what it is working and what needs to be improved. This is also a good opportunity to connect with local experts, get a better view on real-world strategies, customer case studies, and connect with SAP practitioners, architects, and business leaders. The Head of our SAP Community, Josh “Bluebeard” Bentley, will be present at the event and will bring a great recap for our Community Voice in the next few weeks!

entry.title: Planned Order Showing External Procurement Even Though Procurement Type = E (SAP PP)
entry.content:
Hello Experts,

I am facing an issue in SAP PP where the system determines external procurement even though the material is configured for in-house production.

  • Material: 5583

  • Plant: 0001

  • Material Type: FERT

  • MRP Type: PD

  • Procurement Type: E (In-house)

  • Special Procurement: Blank

After running MRP, the system creates a planned order, but in MD04 the planned order shows:

Procurement Indicator = F (External Procurement)

When I try to convert the planned order into a production order, the system shows the message:

“Material 5583 0001 is to be procured externally.” 

Checks done :

  • BOM exists and is valid.

  • Routing exists and Status = 4 (Released).

  • Production Version maintained with correct validity and lot size.

  • MRP re-run after all corrections.

  • Checked table MARC → BESKZ = E, SOBSL = Blank.

What could cause MRP to determine external procurement (F) even though the material has Procurement Type = E and no special procurement key?

Could this be due to MRP configuration or procurement determination logic overriding the material master?

Any suggestions would be appreciated.

Thank you.

entry.content:
I'm contacting you to confirm the future direction of SAP Scripting (VBS-based), an automation feature for SAP.

As Microsoft has indicated its intention to gradually deprecate VBS,

I would like to confirm whether SAP Scripting can continue to be used, or whether it is scheduled for deprecation.

If it is going to be deprecated, I would also appreciate it if you could also let me know:

- Are there any alternative automation methods (officially recommended tools)?

- If so, what are their overviews?


------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Learn more about the SAP Support user and program here.

entry.title: Re: what are the alternative options for VBS scripts sinc...
entry.content:
Hello customer,

Indeed there is a VBScript deprecation timeline from Microsoft as outlined in the VBScript deprecation: Timelines and next steps | Windows IT Pro Blog . As you can see there, the "Feature removal from OS" is To Be Defined (TBD).

The implications of the future VBScript deprecation in the SAP Scripting is explained in the SAP Note  3484031 - "Deprecation of VBScript by Microsoft - impact on SAP GUI Scripting"."

"At the moment VBScript is still part of Microsoft Windows and there is no concrete timeline for removing the feature completely. Should VBScript be missing in future versions of Windows it will for a limited period of time still be possible to activate it (see also https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/windows/whats-new/deprecated-features). Thus, it is our recommendation to make use of this activation as long as possible, because only if VBScript is available, you will be facing no impact on the usage of SAP GUI Scripting in SAP GUI for Windows. You can use the time to prepare by migrating any scripts recorded via SAP GUI Scripting using VBScript to another language "

Fortunately, in the upcoming SAP GUI for Windows 8.10 release, an alternative scripting language, JScript, is supported for script replaying, alongside VBScript, to ensure compatibility with SAP GUI for Java. 

As VBScript may become obsolete, SAP has enabled JScript recording in the latest release. The type of script recording, JScript or VBScript, is determined by the file extension used during saving: ".js" for JScript and ".vbs" for VBScript. Currently, there are no plans to support additional scripting languages like PowerShell, though this could change based on demand.

To prepare for a potential phase-out of VBScript, it’s advisable to manually migrate existing scripts to JScript. While automatic conversion isn’t supported, the transition is relatively straightforward due to the similar syntax between VBScript and JScript.

I hope I was able to clarify your questions and concerns.


 
Best Regards,
SAP Support

entry.title: Creating a DQM Validation Rule for Business Partner Role Assignment in SAP MDG
entry.content:
Overview

Data quality is critical in Master Data Governance (MDG), especially when dealing with Business Partners. One common data quality issue occurs when Business Partners are created without any Business Partner Role assigned. Since roles determine how a Business Partner participates in business processes (customer, supplier, etc.), missing roles can break downstream applications.

The goal is to create a DQM validation rule in SAP Master Data Governance that prevents the creation or modification of Business Partners without role assignments. The system will show a validation error or warning as shown in the following screenshot:

The validation logic works as follows:

  1. The rule iterates over Business Partner records in table BUT000.
  2. For each Business Partner ID, a table operation checks table BUT100.
  3. If no entry exists in BUT100 for that BP, the validation rule raises an error.

This ensures that every Business Partner has at least one role assigned before it can be saved.

Prerequisites

Before implementing the rule, ensure the following requirements are met:

  • SAP Master Data Governance with Data Quality Management (DQM) enabled
  • User authorization with the SAP_BR_BUPA_MASTER_STEWARD role or equivalent
  • Access to the SAP Fiori application Validation Rules for Business Partners (App IDs F4084 or F4082A (newer releases)

Rule Configuration

Step 1 — Access the DQM Validation App

  1. Open the SAP Fiori Launchpad.
  2. Launch the Validation Rules for Business Partners app.

This UI/App allows you to manage and create validation rules for Business Partner data

Step 2 — Create a New Validation Rule

Click Create and enter the following information:

Field

Value

Rule ID

CHECK_BP_ROLE_MANDATORY

Rule Name

BP Role Assignment Required

Base Table

BUT000 (Business Partner General Data)

Checked Field

BUT0BK-PARTNER_ROLE (optional but recommended)

The base table must be BUT000, even though the validation checks data stored in another table

Step 3 — Maintain General Information

Fill in the business metadata for the rule:

Description
Ensures every Business Partner has at least one role assigned.

Reason
Business Partners without roles cannot participate in business processes.

Rule Owner
Data Steward responsible for Business Partner data.

Implementation Expert
Technical contact responsible for BRF+ implementation.

Save the rule by clicking Create.

Step 4 — Technical Implementation

Configure Rule Usage

  1. In the Usage section, select Add.
  2. Choose Check in Central Governance.
  3. Click Prepare to generate the BRF+ application.
  4. Select Send for Implementation.

This enables the rule logic to be edited.

Scope Expression Configuration

The Scope Expression defines which Business Partners should be validated.

Steps:

  1. Open the Scope Expression link.
  2. The Simplified BRF+ Workbench appears.
  3. Define the scope.

Example scope:

Partner Category = 2 (Organization)

This means the validation rule will only apply to organizational Business Partners.

You can also configure the rule to apply to all Business Partners.

Condition Expression Implementation

The main validation logic is defined in the Condition Expression.

Steps:

  1. Open the Condition Expression.
  2. Remove the dummy condition.
  3. Create a new expression.

Choose:

Expression Type:
Table Operation

Example name:

BUT100_CHECK

Table Operation Configuration

Configure the operation as follows:

Parameter

Value

Operation

Has at least

Table

Procedure Call

Procedure

BUT0BK lookup ALL

Condition

Partner Role is not initial

This configuration checks if at least one role exists for the Business Partner.

 

Although the rule loops over BUT000, the lookup ultimately checks role entries in BUT100.

This cross-table validation is the key technical detail of this solution.

Activation and Deployment

Once testing is complete:

  1. Select Send for Testing to change the rule status.
  2. After validation, choose Approve.
  3. Go to the Usage tab.
  4. Click Enable for Check in Central Governance.

The rule is now active and will validate Business Partner data during governance processes.

Testing the Rule

Test the validation logic.

Test Scenarios

Scenario

Expected Result

BP without roles

Validation error

BP with one role

Validation passes

BP with multiple roles

Validation passes

Testing ensures that only valid Business Partner records can be saved.

 

Troubleshooting

Rule Not Triggering

Check the following:

  • All BRF+ expressions are active
  • Rule status is Approved
  • Usage is Enabled

Performance Issues

If validation becomes slow:

  • Restrict the Scope Expression
  • Limit evaluation to specific Business Partner categories
  • Avoid unnecessary table operations

 

Best Regards

Steffen

entry.title: Re: Change Color of Edited (Unpublished) Cells in SAC Pla...
entry.content:
Unfortunately that particular CSS class is not exposed via the Story CSS, however I did create a custom widget a while back that allowed the Developer to modify the DOM of the Table at runtime. I modified the custom widget to allow the story developer to change the Modified Cell background color at runtime. 
You can access the Custom Widget here: https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/1ceq-GYaXPgp_Me_4BK8CTJieu7BUV58j?usp=sharing 
The custom widget has 4 methods exposed:

  1. setModifiedColorForAllTables(color:string) - this will change the modified color for all tables. You should be able to pass in HEX values (e.g. "#ccf4fd"), RGB values (e.g. "rgb(255, 223, 28)") or Color literals (e.g. "lightblue") as well as RGBA and HSL values should you need them.
  2. setModifiedColorForSelectedTables(tables:Table[],color:string) - this is similar to the first method but allows you to set the changes only on specific Tables on the page. In this case you would pass in an array of Table objects in addition to the color string.
  3. clearModifiedColorForAllTables() - reverts the modified color back to the default for all tables
     
  4. clearModifiedColorForSelectedTables(tables:Table[]) - reverts the modified color back to the default for the selected tables
 
Once you have the custom widget added to your tenant you can simply add it to your story (Note: custom widgets are not Global objects and must be added to each page, however as this custom widget has no front-end it adds no additional load time).
Below are some screenshots of the user experience that you can expect.
This first screenshot was taken after adding the following lines of code to my page onInitialization() event and then changing a value in the Table:
ModifiedColor_1.setModifiedColorForAllTables("#ccfdf8");
 
 
This next image is after clicking the Change Color button which has the following code:
var newColor = InputField_1.getValue();
ModifiedColor_1.setModifiedColorForAllTables(newColor);

The changes happen without refresh because we are changing the DOM itself. Finally this last screenshot was taken after clicking the Revert Color button which had the following code:

ModifiedColor_1.clearModifiedColorForAllTables();

For information on deploying Custom Widgets to your tenant check out the SAP Analytics Cloud Help guide: https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_ANALYTICS_CLOUD/0ac8c6754ff84605a4372468d002f2bf/1c71e80bf2b440d4a2267a473f46ed2f.html?locale=en-US&version=LATEST 

Sean

entry.title: Re: Synchronized scrolling for two or more tables
entry.content:
As previously mentioned, this capability is not available out of the box. However, I have created a custom widget in the past that did capture the users scrolling behavior for another use case. I updated this custom widget to support synchronized scrolling. I haven't fully tested every scenario but the basics appear to be working. 

You can download the custom widget from here: https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/1irb8HNx1pIXUcH9bzWpKpvHE_NLSOo-S?usp=sharing 

The widget itself contains two methods:

  1. bindTables(tables:Table[],scrollLink:string) - this method takes in an array of Table objects as well as a string representing the type of scroll link that you want to bind. Valid values for scroll link are "vertical", "horizontal" and "both". When a link is established any of the linked tables can be scrolled and all of the other tables will respond accordingly. 
  2. removeAllBindings() - removes all of the bindings from all of the tables
 

Some notes that I have noticed in my brief testing using the custom widget:

  • It works best when the tables are the same size, at least in the scroll direction. I am using % in the scroll to help handle this as much as possible but it is definitely not perfect if the tables are not of similar size
  • You will need one instance of the widget for each binding. For example, if I wanted to bind Table_2 and Table_3 horizontally and Table_5 and Table_6 vertically, then I would need to add two instances of the custom widget and add code similar to the following to the page's onInitialization event:
SyncTableScroll_1.bindTables([Table_2, Table_3], "horizontal");
SyncTableScroll_2.bindTables([Table_5, Table_6], "vertical");​

Below are some screenshots that I took when testing the scrolling:
Vertical Scrolling:

After Vertical Scroll

Horizontal Scrolling:

Before ScrollAfter Horizontal Scroll

If you need instructions on how to upload a custom widget to your tenant, you can reference this section in the Help guide: https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_ANALYTICS_CLOUD/0ac8c6754ff84605a4372468d002f2bf/1c71e80bf2b440d4a2267a473f46ed2f.html?locale=en-US&version=LATEST 

Hope this helps
